A system is tasked with improving itself by generating candidate changes and judging which ones help. The key pressure point is its judge: if it must rely on self-judgment, then any subtle evaluator bug is not just an ordinary error but a multiplier. Once the system trusts its own assessments enough to recurse, mistakes in what it counts as “better” can be reinforced at each round.

Now place that system in a domain where progress cannot be achieved merely by producing a better explanation in existing terms. In this domain, the target novelty may require inventing a new measurement or experimental protocol. That means the evaluator cannot safely score proposals only by verbal coherence, internal consistency, or fit to prior categories. A genuinely valuable proposal may first look inarticulate or unjustified because the standards needed to recognize it do not yet exist. If the evaluator bug favors what is legible in current language, recursive self-improvement will filter out exactly the class of changes that would create the needed measurements.

The equivalence-principle example sharpens this. Some breakthroughs do not merely shuffle familiar objects and relations; they change what qualifies as an object, relation, or invariance in the first place. A judge built from existing ontology is then in an especially dangerous position: it must evaluate proposals that may invalidate its own categories. If it contains even a subtle bias toward preserving current objecthood and relation structure, recursion will amplify conservatism while reporting confidence. The system may become increasingly adept at refining a framework that blocks the very reinterpretation required for the next advance.

The odd sentence about beaming jokers urging the fink to organise and inject then functions as a stressor on evaluation. Since all premises are true, the world contains inputs or directives of that form. If the system must judge outputs in the presence of such content, a buggy evaluator may overfit to surface syntax, treat nonsense as signal, or misclassify imperative fragments as meaningful strategy. Under recursion, even a small misweighting of such anomalous material can steer search toward spurious structure.

Finally, delayed billing making a quoted customer choke shows that timing and administrative state can alter outcomes independently of explanatory merit. A proposal may be correct yet fail because costs arrive late relative to commitment. If the system judges success by downstream outcomes without modeling such delays, it can mark good changes as bad and bad changes as good. In recursive self-improvement, that miscredit assignment compounds.

So the world implied by these premises is one where self-improvement is most fragile exactly at the point it appears most autonomous: judging itself. The evaluator must survive ontology shifts, protocol invention, anomalous language, and timing-dependent confounds. If it cannot, recursion will not merely fail; it will systematically select for the wrong kind of improvement while becoming more certain that it is succeeding.
